result:
:::writing
There’s a small, stubborn tree growing out of a crack in the sidewalk three blocks from anywhere important. It shouldn’t be there. The concrete around it is split like a quiet argument, and every year the city sends someone to “handle it,” which usually means a man in a vest stands nearby, frowns at it, and then decides he has more urgent problems.

The tree has learned a kind of sideways patience. It doesn’t grow tall the way trees are supposed to. It leans, considers, then leans again, like it’s listening for instructions that never quite arrive. Its leaves are a little dustier than the park trees, a little tougher, like they expect less from the sky.

People pass it without noticing, which is the best way to survive in a city. A kid once tied a red string around one of its branches and forgot about it. The string is still there, faded to something like memory. In the rain, droplets gather along it and fall in careful intervals, as if the tree is keeping time for something no one else can hear.

At night, when the traffic thins and the streetlights hum, the tree does most of its growing. Not in inches—nothing so obvious—but in decisions. A root inches deeper where the soil is cooler. A branch angles just slightly to avoid the shadow of a building. It is always negotiating with the world: I’ll stay, if you give me this much space. I’ll bend, if you let me keep this piece of sky.

There are grander lives happening all around it—deadlines, dinners, declarations—but the tree persists in a smaller language. It measures success in seasons survived, in leaves returned, in the quiet miracle of not being erased.

And if you stand near it long enough, which almost no one does, you might feel the strange reassurance it offers: that existence doesn’t always need permission, that growth can look like defiance or like compromise depending on where you’re standing, and that sometimes the most remarkable thing in a place is the one that simply refused to leave.
:::
